The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated especially in light of recent event with the death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber. Those for self-driving cars argue that they reduce the rate of accidents as a result of distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, are cost saving in terms of time and monetary value from insurance, and could allow greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Those against self-driving cars argue the system does not eliminate accidents, that there is a learning curve with the technology, that it would take away profit from other sectors of the economy, and that there are instances in which the technology is not equipped to make a decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars rises to the forefront. 

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars but it is still in its infancy and has the ability to develop and improve with time whereas we have not seen that in human motorists over the years. Certainly we may expect the number of accidents by self-driving cars to rise as it is more widely adopted but this number palls in comparison to the number of accidents caused by drivers. Tragedies such as those in Arizona should not happen but unfortunately with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents a year and 81 percent of accidents being the result of human error it appears the main culprit is us. As humans we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks and self-driving cars are an extension of the innovative mindset our tech fields have embraced. 

Those against also argue that investing in self-driving cars may take away money from other industries and sectors of the economy. Again while self-driving cars may impact the profits of other transportation system, overall the profits from developing and implementing this technology will outweigh the losses. There is a reason many people invest in tech companies as the have become more profitable in the 21st century. Especially with the start-up of new technology, as they industry grows this will demand that more and more jobs be created to replace the ones lost. Also, savings in terms of reduced accidents are astronomical. The U.S. Department of Transportation has assigned a value of 9.2 million for each human life, with savings coming from venues such as healthcare and insurance. There is also the argument that self-driving cars may also save you time as they free up your transportation time to attend to other daily tasks when you do not have to focus on the driving. With more efficient driving the self-driving cars have the potential to decrease driving time and traffic as well.

Though people are still resistant to the idea of self-driving cars it is already in ways being implemented into our driving with features such as autonomous braking, self-parking and sensor detectors. The technology for this field is currently available and calls on our investment to develop and improve. While no system is perfect, one that saves us lives and on costs should be highly considered.

output_text:
The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated, especially in light of recent events, such as the tragic death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber vehicle. Proponents of self-driving cars argue that they have the potential to significantly reduce the rate of accidents, particularly those caused by distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, offer substantial cost savings in terms of time and insurance, and provide greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Conversely, opponents contend that self-driving technology does not completely eliminate accidents, involves a steep learning curve, threatens jobs in traditional sectors, and sometimes fails to make the right decisions in complex situations. While both sides present compelling arguments, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars emerge as overwhelmingly favorable.

Many critics of self-driving cars are quick to highlight their flaws; however, it is essential to recognize that this technology is still in its infancy and holds the promise of continued development and improvement over time. In stark contrast, human drivers have demonstrated a persistent inability to reduce accident rates despite decades of advancement in automotive safety. While we can anticipate an initial increase in accidents involving self-driving cars as they become more widely adopted, this number pales in comparison to the staggering statistics associated with human-driven vehicles. According to the World Health Organization, approximately 1.3 million people die in motor vehicle accidents each year, with a staggering 81 percent of these incidents attributed to human error. This data suggests that the primary culprit in road safety is, overwhelmingly, human behavior. As a society, we have come to rely on computers and technology for various daily tasks, and self-driving cars represent a natural extension of the innovative mindset that has driven progress in the technology sector.

Opponents of self-driving cars also argue that investment in this technology may siphon funds away from other essential industries and economic sectors. However, while it is true that self-driving cars may disrupt profits in some areas of transportation, the overall economic benefits derived from developing and implementing this technology are likely to outweigh the losses. This assertion is supported by the growing trend of investment in technology companies, which have proven to be among the most profitable sectors of the 21st century. Furthermore, the emergence of new technologies often generates job creation to offset those lost, as industries evolve and adapt to changing market demands. Additionally, the savings associated with reduced accident rates are astronomical. The U.S. Department of Transportation assigns a value of approximately $9.2 million to each human life, which highlights the substantial economic impact of preventing fatalities and injuries. Savings can be realized across various sectors, including healthcare and insurance. Moreover, self-driving cars can save valuable time for individuals, allowing them to engage in other daily activities while the car handles the driving. The potential for more efficient driving could lead to reduced travel times and alleviated traffic congestion.

Despite the resistance some individuals have toward the concept of self-driving cars, elements of this technology are already being integrated into our vehicles, as evidenced by features such as autonomous braking, self-parking, and advanced sensor detection systems. The foundation for this field is already established, and further investments are crucial for its development and refinement. While no system can be deemed perfect, the potential benefits of self-driving cars—including the preservation of lives and significant cost savings—should be considered with the utmost seriousness. In conclusion, the transition to self-driving vehicles is not merely a technological upgrade; it represents a transformative shift that could redefine personal transportation, enhance road safety, and ultimately lead to a more efficient society.

draft1:
Self-driving car is a new technology and receives a lot of debates recently. On one hand, self-driving cars offer positive impacts, e.g. safer commuting, cost savings, and give opportunities to many people who can't travel by themselves at this moment. On the other hand, people also raise concerns about the security, safety, and economy of the technology.
In my opinion, I support bringing self-driving cars into reality. The technology can bring many benefits to humanity. First, we, as human in modern societies, will save a lot of money thanks to the technology. Because when a lot of self-driving cars are on the street and they can communicate with each other, they can reduce the problem with congestion or unknown traffic conditions. Second, self-driving cars are supposed to be more safety because it can eliminate mistakes made by a human. The most common mistake is driving under the influence (of alcohol, drugs, or tireness). Moreover, drivers tend to have false confidence as they have more driving hours. This false confidence will increase the chances that an accident will happen. Last but not least, self-driving cars put an equity for people having difficulties in traveling, such as old people or disabilities. While public transportations are available in all cities, in many cases, they can only take a person to a pre-designated location (stop) rather than the real destination. Public transportations are operated with a fixed schedule which does not perfectly match everyone's situations. 
The cons about self-driving cars could be categorized into two groups: not-ready and not-true. The not-ready group contains arguments about new challenges that the technology will bring in when it is deployed in mass. For example, when an accident is made by a self-driving car, who will hold the responsibility? Or security issues such as how can drivers protect their personal data and what will happen if the car is hijacked by a hi-tech criminal? Interestingly, what would the car-makers or software designers choose in moral-sensitive situations? For example, given an unavoidable accident will happen, which will either kill an old pedestrian crossing the street or a baby in a stroller on the pavement, which choice should be made by the self-driving car? These issues must be addressed or disclosed before deploying self-driving cars in the mass. The not-true group consists of false assumptions about the self-driving technology. For example, there are concerns about drivers are no longer equipped with the skills to operate cars. In fact, we are living well in a society without knowing much about our daily stuffs. I don't know anything about fixing a car, but I can always call AAA or my friends whenever my car breaking down. I use the washing machine at least twice a week without knowing how to make such a machine. Another false opinion is the difference between states regarding turning right on red or cars cannot interpret police officers' signals when directing traffics. In fact, the car can update its algorithm according to the state it is traveling simply based on GPS signal. Moreover, when all cars on the streets are self-driving cars, the police officer does not need to go down the street and directs the traffic but just sends information to the car through a communication channel, e.g. 3G or wifi. Last but not least, some people even afraid that auto manufacturers might sell fewer cars. This is not true as a self-driving car is still a car which needs a manufacturer. In fact, we have seen more and more car manufacturers join the self-driving race nowadays, such as Volvo, BMW, Toyota, Honda, and many more.
In conclusion, I think self-driving car is a revolution that will have more benefit to our society. However, there are still some concerns needed to be addressed in order to make this technology available to everyone.

output_text:
Self-driving cars represent a groundbreaking technology that has ignited extensive debate in recent years. On one hand, proponents highlight the numerous positive impacts of self-driving vehicles, such as safer commuting, cost savings, and increased mobility for individuals who currently face challenges in traveling independently. On the other hand, critics express concerns surrounding security, safety, and the economic implications of this emerging technology.

In my opinion, I wholeheartedly support the integration of self-driving cars into our society. The technology promises to deliver significant benefits that could enhance the quality of life for many. First and foremost, self-driving cars have the potential to drastically reduce commuting costs. As these vehicles become more prevalent and capable of communicating with one another, they can effectively mitigate traffic congestion and optimize routes in real-time. This not only saves time but also reduces fuel consumption and vehicle wear, ultimately leading to substantial financial savings for consumers and municipalities alike.

Safety is another paramount advantage of self-driving technology. Automating the driving process can significantly reduce accidents caused by human error, which remains the leading cause of road fatalities. Common mistakes include driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, as well as errors stemming from fatigue or distracted driving. Studies indicate that even experienced drivers often suffer from overconfidence, which can lead to misjudgments and increased accident risk. By removing the human element from driving, self-driving cars can minimize these dangerous behaviors and create a safer environment on our roads.

Moreover, self-driving cars represent a leap toward greater equity in transportation. Many individuals, such as the elderly or those with disabilities, face significant barriers when it comes to mobility. While public transportation systems exist in urban areas, they often fail to provide door-to-door service and operate on rigid schedules that do not accommodate everyone’s needs. Self-driving cars can fill this gap, providing personalized and flexible transportation options that empower individuals who might otherwise struggle to travel independently.

The concerns surrounding self-driving cars can be categorized into two main groups: those based on readiness and those based on misconceptions. The "not-ready" group encompasses valid questions about the challenges that this technology may introduce once it is widely deployed. For instance, in the event of an accident involving a self-driving car, who would be held responsible? Additionally, there are pressing security concerns, such as how to safeguard personal data from potential cyber threats and what measures will be in place to prevent hijacking by tech-savvy criminals. Furthermore, ethical dilemmas arise in situations where a self-driving car must make a split-second decision in a life-threatening scenario, such as choosing between the safety of an elderly pedestrian or a child in a stroller. These complex issues necessitate thorough consideration and transparent dialogue before self-driving cars can be safely integrated into society.

The "not-true" group consists of misconceptions about self-driving technology that often cloud the discussion. A prevalent concern is that human drivers will lose their skills due to reliance on automation. However, we already live in a world where many people navigate daily life without comprehensive knowledge of all the underlying technologies, such as calling for roadside assistance when a vehicle breaks down or using a washing machine without understanding its mechanics. The fear that self-driving cars will struggle with regional driving laws, like turning right on red or interpreting police signals, is also unfounded. With advancements in GPS technology and real-time data sharing, self-driving cars can adapt their algorithms to comply with local regulations seamlessly. In a future where all vehicles are self-driving, communication between law enforcement and vehicles could be streamlined, allowing officers to relay traffic instructions directly to cars via secure channels like 3G or Wi-Fi.

Lastly, some individuals express concern that the rise of self-driving cars may lead to reduced sales for auto manufacturers. This misconception overlooks the fact that self-driving cars are still fundamentally vehicles that require manufacturers for production. In reality, the race to develop self-driving technology has seen an influx of investment from major car manufacturers, including Volvo, BMW, Toyota, and Honda, all eager to participate in this transformative shift in the automotive industry.

In conclusion, I firmly believe that self-driving cars represent a revolutionary advancement that can yield significant benefits for society. However, it is crucial to address the concerns and challenges associated with this technology to ensure its safe and equitable implementation for all. By fostering open dialogue and collaboration among stakeholders, we can pave the way for a future where self-driving cars enhance our lives in unprecedented ways.

Self-driving cars, though only recently becoming a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development and have thoroughly captured the public eye. The debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole is raging onward with impassioned argument on both sides. In this essay I will aim to provide convincing evidence that though it is not perfect, the prospect of driver-less roads would mark a definitive improvement over our current conditions. 

To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. Although it is not yet certain that self-driving cars in their current condition would cause that number to go down, the lack of human fallibility makes the prospect seem likely. Furthermore, whereas human driver tend to fall into a lull of false confidence when they have engaged in a task for an extended period of time, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. There is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. 

Furthermore, self-driving cars could give society a method of transportation which is much less restrictive. Currently, driving privileges are limited to those capable of operating the machinery, passing their test, and -for the most part - affording to own their own vehicle. If a child needs to be transported to any sort of extracurricular event they are bound by their parents' schedules and mobilities. If a senior citizen who has been deemed unsafe to drive decides to go shopping they must wait for long periods to use public transportation with conditions that could be extremely painful or uncomfortable with those who experience chronic pain from arthritis or similar conditions. Furthermore, many who cannot afford their own car are forced to commute long distances to their jobs, health-care, and programs, forcing a de facto restriction on who may access what. Although it is likely that one who cannot afford a regular car could not afford their own self-driving car, there is nothing to say that they must have their own. The ability to have a car drive back to the place it left from could allow a much more intricate form of car sharing; additionally, the current restrictions on insured drivers that hassle any type of shared vehicle would likely be thoroughly relaxed or missing entirely for a driver-less car. Even beyond the prospect of shared cars, self-driving vehicles could revolutionize public transportation, making a wider range of pick up locations and drop off locations feasible. If large buses or a huge fleet of public cars were to have flexible routes that could give people who do not have the means to own their own car a transportational freedom akin to it at a fraction of the price that services like Uber and Lift offer today. 

A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine much of their success and failure. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it does not seem to be a stretch to claim that this could bring us dramatic improvements.

Self-driving cars, though only recently having become a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development, managing to thoroughly capture the public eye. A debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole has been raging onward with impassioned arguments on both sides. In this essay I aim to provide convincing evidence that, though they have potential to cause societal harm, given the distressing array of negative societal impacts associated with human-driven cars, the apparent feasibility for self-driving cars to ameliorate these issues makes it only rational to welcome their implementation. 
	To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these, it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. 
	While no one would rightly argue that our current system is ideal, skepticism toward self-driving cars at this stage is more than fair. The development of the vehicles is very much still in its developmental infancy. Coupling that fact with the reality of a mixed automotive environment - with both computerized and human driven cars - one might very well expect implementation of self-driving cars to cause an increase in accidents. The key question then becomes: "would a self-driving car actually be any safer than a human-driven one?". While there is currently insufficient information to give a definite answer, I think there are good reasons to put more faith in the self-driving cars. Whereas human drivers tend to fall into a lull of false confidence after driving for many years, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. Further, there is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. In the absence of such risks, a computerized car might very well behave safer even without the ability to intuitively grasp tendencies and perceptions of neighboring human drivers. 
	Others however, are more concerned about the societal impact of these cars than their safety specs. Even if the change means less lives lost on the road, it still may be an unwise one if it leads to the ruin of lives off the road. Given the number of transportation jobs, a computerized revolution could displace the occupations of many currently employed drivers. Furthermore, given the massive economic investment needed to own such a car, might this lead to a widening of already egregious inequality gaps in the country? 
	The transportation industry represents a real threat if we have no place for the newly unemployed to turn. This however, is not a problem unique to self-driving cars but rather, is a pervasive and imminent threat encroaching on us from all directions given the rapid advance of technological competency. If we cannot, as a society solve this more generally, the threats will be realized even in the absence of the driver-less car revolution; if we do find a way to support the displaced workers of other sectors I remain hopeful that such approaches can be implemented to the transportation-sector as well. 
	As for societal inequality, self-driving vehicles, if implemented properly, could actually be a great boon to economically struggling classes. To begin, the previously considered transportation industry automation, though bad for workers, could be fantastic for commodity prices. If the cost of all daily goods dropped, many would be the better for it. Further, current systems of public transportation are woefully insufficient to serve their desired societal role. When jobs, hospitals, and enrichment events are inconvenient to access, attendance from under serviced individuals tends to drop. This creates a de-facto restriction on who may access what. With an automated public transportation system, it could be much easier to introduce flexible pick-up locations, drop-off spots, and schedules. With a more functional and convenient public transportation system, we could relieve both the economic burden of buying a vehicle as well as the logistical burden of navigating tricky and time-consuming bus routes. 
	A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine whether those differences were successes or failures. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it is reasonable to maintain optimism about the ability of self-driving cars to bring about dramatic improvements to our lives.

However, when we weigh these concerns against the massive limitations and problems with our current system—such as traffic fatalities, accessibility issues, and the environmental impact of traditional driving—it does not seem a stretch to claim that self-driving cars could bring us dramatic improvements. By addressing these challenges thoughtfully and proactively, we can harness the potential of autonomous vehicles to create a safer, more efficient, and more inclusive transportation system for all.

There are many advantages for the United States to transition to self-driving cars. The primary benefit is the significant reduction in fatalities and injuries due to the elimination of intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Statistics reveal that around 33,000 people die in automobile accidents every year in the U.S., and a substantial portion of these tragedies could be prevented. Self-driving cars, equipped with advanced sensors and algorithms, provide a precise and reliable alternative to the potential for human error that arises each time a driver gets behind the wheel. By removing the unpredictable element of human behavior from the equation, we can create a safer driving environment for everyone.

In addition to saving lives, self-driving cars can lead to substantial economic benefits. For instance, insurance costs could decrease significantly as accident rates drop, which would also alleviate the burden on our outdated infrastructure that struggles to accommodate the growing number of vehicles on the road. Furthermore, these vehicles have the potential to reduce traffic congestion, as they can communicate with each other to optimize driving patterns and routes. From an environmental perspective, self-driving cars can contribute to a decrease in emissions, particularly if they are electric. By promoting more efficient driving behaviors—such as maintaining optimal speeds and minimizing unnecessary idling—we can work towards a more sustainable future.

However, the journey towards widespread adoption of self-driving cars is not without challenges. The transition to these vehicles poses significant hurdles, including the initial high cost of technology, the reliability of the systems, and the potential job loss in industries such as trucking and taxi services. These limitations cannot be ignored; they require careful consideration and strategic planning to ensure that the implementation of self-driving technology benefits society as a whole.

Frequently, we hear arguments for or against a particular advancement with the justification that it benefits or does not benefit society. In order to make an informed argument, it is imperative to consider critical questions: Who is society? What constitutes the good for society? What does society need? And at what expense to the individual do we support the public good? In the case of self-driving cars, the answer to these questions is clear. Anything that has the potential to save human lives is both good for the individual and good for society. Despite the limitations in technology, the initial costs, and the likely challenging transition, self-driving cars represent the future of transportation, and they will undeniably save lives. 

Our driving problem in America is not just serious; it is a public health crisis. The staggering number of annual deaths from vehicular accidents should prompt immediate action. The fact that these fatalities are often preventable—due to factors like distractions, alcohol consumption, fatigue, or recklessness—highlights the urgent need for a safer alternative. Moreover, vehicle accidents are the leading cause of death for teenagers, a demographic particularly prone to risky behaviors such as impaired or reckless driving. It is time to reconsider our association with traditional driving practices that have led to such senseless destruction and loss of life.

Interestingly, the Department of Transportation has calculated that each life lost in an accident has an economic value of $9.2 million. While this figure attempts to quantify the tragic consequences of loss, it fundamentally fails to capture the true value of a human life. A human life is priceless, and no amount of money can restore a loved one who has been killed in a preventable accident.

If you have reservations about self-driving cars due to the potential for technological failure, I share those concerns with you. Recent incidents, such as the tragic case in Arizona where a self-driving car struck and killed a woman crossing the street outside of a crosswalk, highlight the imperfections of current technology. We have not perfected this system; there are still miles to go before it can be implemented on a large scale. Ethical, legal, and policy considerations must be addressed to ensure the safe integration of this technology into our daily lives. 

However, despite these challenges, we have an incredible opportunity to save countless lives and prevent debilitating injuries. We have the obligation to harness technology as it has always been intended—to benefit our society and improve the lives of all Americans. Embracing self-driving cars is not just a technological advancement; it is a moral imperative that could redefine our relationship with transportation and significantly enhance public safety.

output_text:
When *Back to the Future I* and *II* were released in the 1980s, many people were captivated by the futuristic technology depicted in the films. The notion of flying cars and floating hoverboards seemed like a whimsical dream, far removed from reality. However, as we approach 2020, the realm of advanced technology is becoming increasingly plausible, and the idea of self-driving cars is transitioning from science fiction to a tangible reality. Olivier Garret, in a Forbes article, predicted that there would be 10 million self-driving cars on the road by 2020, with estimates suggesting that one out of every four cars could be autonomous by 2030. Although *Back to the Future II* envisioned flying cars by 2015, the arrival of self-driving cars is not as far-fetched as it once seemed, indicating a shift in how we perceive transportation.

In recent years, major automotive companies such as Ford, Honda, BMW, and Tesla have been at the forefront of developing self-driving technology. This push towards automation makes sense, given the rapid advancements in technology and the growing demand for solutions that can enhance our daily lives. As we delve into the potential of self-driving cars, it is crucial to weigh both the advantages and disadvantages of this innovation. The landscape of autonomous vehicles is still emerging, and understanding these factors is essential as society prepares for a future where self-driving cars may dominate the roads.

The highlights of self-driving cars are compelling. Firstly, the reduction of human error is a significant advantage; these vehicles are designed to adhere strictly to traffic laws and regulations, minimizing accidents caused by distracted or impaired driving. Furthermore, with the automation of driving tasks, passengers will have the opportunity to engage in other activities, effectively multitasking and enhancing productivity during their commutes. Additionally, self-driving technology has the potential to streamline transportation systems overall, leading to improved traffic flow and reduced congestion. As a proponent of technological advancement, I believe the benefits of self-driving cars outweigh the disadvantages. The swift pace of innovation necessitates that our automobiles evolve to keep up with societal needs.

However, it is understandable that there are significant concerns surrounding the implementation of autonomous vehicles. One major issue is the potential impact on employment; professions such as driver's education instructors, DMV personnel, and rideshare drivers from companies like Uber and Lyft may face job reductions as self-driving cars become more prevalent. This displacement could lead to increased unemployment rates, a legitimate concern that society must address as we transition to a new era of transportation.

Moreover, the possibility of technology failures, such as software glitches or malfunctions, raises vital safety concerns. Autonomous vehicles may experience accidents that could be more severe than those involving human drivers, prompting questions about liability and insurance. As the market for self-driving cars expands, insurance companies may adapt by creating new policies specifically tailored to these vehicles. This could result in higher premiums for consumers, as insurers seek to mitigate risks associated with potential technological failures. The prospect of increased insurance costs—potentially by $200-300 per month—is daunting, especially considering the already significant financial burden of auto insurance.

In conclusion, self-driving cars are rapidly making their way into our society, presenting a complex array of positive and negative implications. While there are numerous advantages to this technological advancement, including enhanced safety and efficiency, there are also pressing concerns that require further exploration and resolution. Personally, as someone who dislikes driving and harbors a fear of accidents, I would readily opt for a self-driving car over a conventional vehicle. As we stand on the brink of this automotive revolution, it is crucial to engage in ongoing dialogue and research to navigate the challenges and opportunities that lie ahead.

The cars can make it safe for people with disabilities to run errands without needing to call a friend or a cab. They will reduce the number of traffic fatalities, especially those caused by driver inattention or inebriation. There are plenty of valid concerns about rolling self-driving cars into the market today but tomorrow is another story. Their use will ultimately benefit their passengers and the environment by creating a safer world.

The world is on the brink of environmental disaster. Irreparable damage has already be done, but further harm can be minimized through the advancement of many technologies, self-driving cars are one. Beyond the greater fuel efficiency self-driving cars provide, there is one feature that people do not often consider. Self-driving cars do not need to be owned by an individual. They can be owned by company, such as Zip Car or Uber. Consumers can pay these companies a monthly fee to have a car available to them whenever they need it and they can also opt in to a car pool so that the car picks up others going to the same place they are. Many people drive a car to work and home again, but they do not use the car throughout the day and they certainly don't use it while they are asleep. During times of non-use, the car could be picking up other riders. This would reduce the number of cars on the road, parked on the sides of streets, and adding to the carbon footprint.

There are valid concerns to this. People worry about the loss of jobs associated with self-driving cars as taxi drivers and truck drivers may become a job of the past. However, the job market is constantly evolving. It was not long ago when cars did not exist, ergo these jobs did not exist. The future will bring new fields for people to work in and new streams of revenue. Change is daunting, and many people are nervous about how a world with self-driving cars will function, but there is no question that it will function. draft3:
Self-driving cars have arrived and are on the market even if their drivers wouldn't define them as such. Drivers are using cars that will auto park, brake if they approach an obstruction, and follow a car in front of them. They may not feel like they are self-driving as they do still require some human interaction and drivers can opt-in to such features, but when they are all used in combination, the car is in effect driving itself. Even when not in control of the wheel, these features can alert drivers to cars in their blind spot and provide a rear view when the driver is in moving backwards. Whether or not people want this future is now irrelevant; it's coming and soon people will be so used to it that they won’t remember what it was like to drive without a little backup support. Eventually, they won’t remember what it was like to drive at all, and that’s a good thing. Everyone will be safer when computers take the wheel.
	People are terrible drivers. We as humans like to believe that we can stay awake, pay attention, and maneuver an enormous hunk of metal at high speeds. The reality is that even when focused on the road, the human brain can only absorb a limited amount of its surroundings. People worry that self-driving cars are not as good as humans at detecting pedestrians or other anomalies, and they aren't as good--they're better at it. Computers have the capability to process the entire environment, not just what's in their fovea or what's left over after the brain has filtered out what it considers irrelevant. And they do it without getting drowsy or lost in their thoughts. Self-driving cars have years of clocked hours on the road. In all of those hours, only one pedestrian fatality has been caused by a self-driving car in the United States. On the other hand, 23 cyclists were killed in 2017 by human-driven cars in New York City alone (CBS New York). Clearly self-driving cars already have a better track record than their predecessors. 
	However, people should not be surrendering unmonitored control of the driving to their car just yet. There are some edges to human cognition that self-driving cars do not currently share even if they surpass it on others. One of them is the knowledge that some caution should be taken when accelerating around turns or at the peak of hills. I have been in cars that are set to regulate their speed to conditions of the road and the drivers around them, but on a nice day with no other cars in sight of the cameras, they will ramp up speed as they reach the peak of the hill without any knowledge of what may be on the other side. Due to the superior reaction times of a computer as compared to a human, the car could likely avoid any unforeseen obstruction that was just at the top and out of sight of the hill, but its passengers would not have enjoyed the abrupt stop. Even without an accident, such an abrupt stop can cause air bags to deploy or cause the body to jerk against the seatbelt and snap the collar bone. Such a need for abrupt stops would have been eliminated by a careful human driver who slowed down in anticipation of what might be lurking just beyond the crest of the hill. 
	The fact that self-driving cars are not ready to chauffeur people around en masse yet does not mean that it’s not coming though or that this reality is anything to fear. These cars just need more training, and unlike people, when one car trains, they all learn. Whether its navigating the Pittsburgh left or learning to be cautious when its camera does not detect straight road ahead, when one car learns, they all learn because they all share the technology. Thankfully, for the consumer, the transition to a self-driving car is a slow one. People will have time to adjust to the new reality and the software engineers will use this time to improve the technology. We have moved from basic cruise control to cars that will match pace with traffic. We have moved from rear-view cameras to cars that parallel park themselves. People are feeling safer about letting their car take over and when consumers are ready to give up the wheel, the car manufacturers will be there with a self-driving version. When that day comes, we’ll all be safer for it, and maybe the number of cyclists killed in New York City in a given year will finally reach zero. 
	CBSNewYork. “NYC Pedestrian Deaths Drop 32 Percent Last Year.” CBS New York, CBS New York, 8 Jan. 2018, newyork.cbslocal.com/2018/01/08/2017-pedestrian-deaths-nyc/.

output_text:
The growth of technology and machines has reached an apex in the 21st century. Humans have ingeniously found ways to automate nearly every conceivable task, leveraging the power of high computing capabilities and sophisticated algorithms. Driving, which has long been both a passion for many and a mundane chore for others, remains a vital skill in our society. In recent years, advancements in road infrastructure have further lessened the effort required for driving. Self-driving cars have the potential to eliminate the repetitive task of driving, offering significant advantages, particularly for taxi drivers and those who view driving as more of a job than a personal choice. However, despite my strong support for research and development in this area, I harbor serious reservations about the widespread implementation of self-driving vehicles.

As a millennial who began driving at an early age, I have witnessed firsthand the evolution of car ownership and the driving experience. The global economy has made cars more affordable, allowing a broader segment of the population to embrace this form of personal freedom. While self-driving technology could drastically reduce the effort involved in driving, its benefits may not be universally applicable. Growing up in the foothills of the majestic Himalayas, I experienced driving in an area where infrastructure is often lacking. The treacherous curves of Himalayan roads demand intense concentration and skill, making driving a considerable challenge. It is evident that self-driving cars, which have been extensively tested and have performed exceptionally well in the United States—renowned for its well-developed infrastructure—may not be suitable for all environments. The success of these autonomous vehicles heavily relies on accurate mapping systems via GPS. Unfortunately, such precision is not universally available and can falter in adverse weather conditions, which poses significant risks for self-driving technology.

Moreover, the reliance on machine learning and the algorithms that train these vehicles introduces another layer of complexity. These systems have not yet reached a level of perfection that guarantees 100% accuracy; thus, their potential for error raises concerns, especially in critical situations where quick decision-making is essential. Another pressing issue is the necessity of having human operators who can adeptly maneuver sophisticated technology. Many individuals, particularly those in middle age or older, may feel intimidated by advanced technological systems. This reluctance to embrace technology can lead to dangerous situations, as hesitance in relinquishing control to a machine may result in malfunctions or misjudgments.

Even if we set aside the concerns related to technology and human adaptability, the financial implications of implementing self-driving cars on a large scale are daunting. The costs associated with developing the necessary infrastructure, alongside the technology itself, are substantial and could outweigh the perceived benefits of such an initiative. Critics of self-driving vehicles often cite their potential to reduce accidents; however, the reality is more complex. Recent incidents involving self-driving cars and their involvement in accidents have sparked skepticism regarding their ability to enhance road safety. A minor malfunction in the system can lead to catastrophic consequences, and the rapidity with which these situations can escalate underscores the importance of having a human operator capable of taking control in emergencies. On the perilous roads of the Himalayas, where drivers must exhibit unwavering focus at every turn, the stakes are even higher, and the risks associated with self-driving cars become starkly evident.

In conclusion, while the concept of self-driving vehicles is undeniably promising, numerous constraints limit their viability, including civil infrastructure, GPS availability, weather conditions, and the significant financial burden of implementation. Although these vehicles may be appropriate in environments that meet all these criteria, the broader application remains fraught with challenges that render widespread adoption unfeasible at this time.

output_text:
Self-driving vehicles will significantly decrease fatalities, reduce commute times, lower expenditures on mass transit, and provide increased transportation options for individuals with disabilities. Each of these benefits directly impacts every member of a community. Transportation-related issues affect a diverse range of people, from those who must wake up earlier to navigate traffic due to increased workloads, to families who receive distressing news about a loved one involved in an accident. The widespread adoption of self-driving vehicles will positively correlate with the daily lives of those who do not own them, creating a ripple effect of improved safety and efficiency throughout society.

Currently, the U.S. Department of Transportation estimates the value of a human life at $9.2 million. This figure underscores the importance of reducing fatalities on the road. Self-driving vehicles, equipped with advanced sensors and algorithms, have the potential to greatly reduce human error—the leading cause of accidents. Studies have shown that a significant percentage of car accidents result from factors such as distracted or impaired driving. By minimizing these risks, self-driving vehicles can lead to a dramatic decrease in road fatalities, which not only saves lives but also alleviates the financial burden on insurance companies. As the prevalence of safer self-driving vehicles increases, insurance premiums are likely to decrease as companies adjust their pricing to remain competitive in a safer environment.

Moreover, as the technology behind self-driving vehicles continues to evolve, the cost of purchasing such vehicles is expected to decline. Automakers are likely to discover more efficient methods of production and distribution as demand grows, leading to economies of scale. This reduction in costs will not only benefit consumers but will also translate into savings for local and state governments. With fewer people relying on traditional mass transit options like city buses and trains, maintenance costs for these systems will plummet. Consequently, taxpayers in these municipalities will benefit from lower public transportation costs, allowing funds to be allocated to other pressing community needs.

However, the rise of self-driving vehicles does come with challenges, particularly concerning employment in certain sectors. The increased availability of autonomous transportation services could diminish the demand for public transportation and taxi services, leading to job losses in these areas. Yet, it is crucial to recognize that the advent of self-driving technology will also create new job opportunities. There will be a heightened demand for skilled workers in fields such as engineering, automotive repair, road infrastructure development, and sales related to autonomous vehicles. This shift in the job market could stimulate economic growth and foster a new workforce skilled in cutting-edge technologies.

Both supporters and opponents of self-driving vehicles have valid concerns regarding their implementation and impact. However, when considering the potential to assign a monetary value to human life, it is essential to explore ways to mitigate the costs associated with fatalities on the road. Self-driving vehicles present a viable solution to this pressing issue. The ongoing development of safety measures within autonomous systems will only improve as consumer adoption increases. Incidents of drunk, tired, and distracted driving are expected to decrease markedly, resulting in safer roads for everyone.

In conclusion, the transition to self-driving vehicles promises to enhance public safety, reduce transportation costs, and create economic opportunities, ultimately leading to a more efficient and safer society. As we navigate the challenges and opportunities presented by this technological advancement, it is imperative to prioritize the well-being of individuals and communities, ensuring that the benefits of self-driving vehicles are realized for all.

The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars", provides an in depth look into the reasoning of whether or not we, as a society, should adopt self-driving vehicles.  Of the 20 "pros" listed, an increase in safety was likely the leading factor, but also included were the benefits in commute times and city travel, the possibility for the driver to do something productive in the time that would otherwise be used for driving, and the idea that elderly or disabled people would be able to ride self-driving cars without worry.  However, in contrast to the advantages listed in the article, the author also offered 20 disadvantages.  Many of these focused on the training involved in learning how to operate it, the high cost, security issues (both personal and cybersecurity), legal ramifications, the fact that a self-driving vehicle will not be able to appropriately respond to all situations and other technical hangups,and the financial implications caused by uprooting an old industry. 

When weighing the "pros" and "cons" in order to gauge whether adopting self-driving cars would be beneficial to society, it is important to look at the whole picture.  The "pros" are all valid, while it appears that many of the "cons" can be addressed as the self-driving vehicles further develop.  For example, the high initial cost is cited as a reason against adopting these vehicles.  However, what isn't taken into consideration in this case is the fact that new technologies typically drastically drop in price over a short amount of time.  We often hear the story of the first computers being the size of an entire room and costing hundreds of thousands of dollars at the time.  However, in a few tens of years the price of those computers came down enough that many families could afford them.  I believe a similar situation could occur with the sensor and computer package required in self-driving vehicles.  As a second example, cybersecurity has become a very hot topic recently and appears in the "cons" against these vehicles.  However, several very critical pieces of infrastructure are operated and they have been largely protected against cybersecurity attacks.  Take for example remote controlled drones currently in use by the military.  The risk of a cyber attack is definitely present, but the designers have found a way to adequately protect the devices.  Something similar could be done for self-driving vehicles.

Although several more examples supporting the idea that many of the present "cons" seen in self-driving vehicle adoption can be corrected in the future, it is unnecessary seeing that they are quite similar.  However, what is important to notice is that all of the "pros" do not seem as if they will be changing, even as the vehicles themselves improve.  Therefore I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

output_text:
The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars" provides an in-depth look into the reasoning behind whether society should adopt self-driving vehicles. Among the 20 "pros" listed, an increase in safety emerges as a leading factor. This is particularly compelling given the alarming statistics surrounding traffic accidents caused by human error. Furthermore, the potential benefits extend beyond mere safety; they include improvements in commute times and city travel efficiency, the opportunity for drivers to engage in productive activities during what would otherwise be spent behind the wheel, and the significant empowerment of elderly or disabled individuals who would gain increased mobility without the anxiety of traditional driving.

However, these advantages are countered by an equal number of disadvantages presented in the article. Many of the cons focus on the complexities involved in learning to operate self-driving technology, the high initial costs associated with the vehicles, security issues—including both personal and cybersecurity threats—legal ramifications of liability in accidents, the challenges of programming vehicles to respond appropriately to unpredictable real-world situations, and the economic upheaval that could arise from the displacement of jobs in the traditional driving sector.

When weighing the "pros" and "cons" to assess whether adopting self-driving cars would be beneficial to society, it is crucial to consider the entire picture. The validity of the pros is evident, while many of the cons appear to be contingent upon the current state of technology rather than insurmountable obstacles. For instance, the high initial cost of self-driving vehicles is frequently cited as a barrier to their adoption. However, this perspective neglects to recognize a common trend in technological advancement: new technologies typically experience a dramatic decline in price over time. A historical parallel can be drawn to the evolution of computers; the first models were massive, room-filling machines that cost hundreds of thousands of dollars. Yet, within a few decades, advancements in technology led to personal computers becoming accessible to a broad demographic. I believe we could witness a similar trajectory with the sensors and computing systems essential for self-driving cars.

Moreover, cybersecurity has emerged as a significant concern in discussions about self-driving vehicles. While the risks of cyberattacks are valid, it is important to note that many critical infrastructures are currently operated remotely and have been effectively safeguarded against potential breaches. For example, military drones operate under an array of cybersecurity measures designed to protect against hacking attempts. This establishes a precedent that suggests similar robust security protocols could be implemented for self-driving vehicles, enhancing their safety and public acceptance.

Although many more examples could further illustrate the idea that several present "cons" associated with self-driving vehicle adoption can be addressed in the future, it may be unnecessary to delve into each one, as they often share similar underlying issues. What is particularly noteworthy, however, is that the advantages—such as increased safety and improved mobility—are likely to remain relevant and beneficial, regardless of technological improvements. Therefore, it is my recommendation that we pursue a gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles in society. This approach allows us to harness the technological advancements that promise to enhance our transportation systems while simultaneously addressing the challenges that accompany such a transformative shift. Through careful implementation, we can work toward a future where self-driving cars contribute positively to our communities, ultimately leading to safer, more efficient, and more inclusive mobility solutions.

Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  The strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, however, is somewhat surprising, given how much safer the technology tends to be.  Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are.

A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet eighty-one percent of vehicular accidents are the result of human error.  Those in working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers.  Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole.  Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with a dizzying array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television.  Self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers.  In a city with limited taxi service and practically no commercial designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to drive from the Southside to the South Hills with often destructive consequences.

Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower income and disabled locals out of the discussion.  Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation.  Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access.

The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles.  With an initial cost of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles.  Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter.  This is alleviated by two considerations, though.  The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth.  What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years.  What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.

Since then, the city has become a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with the mayor keen on attracting and retaining talent at the forefront of this groundbreaking technology. Reactions to these advancements have long been mixed, particularly after a fatal accident involving an automated Uber vehicle in Arizona, which recently halted Uber's automated test drives. The strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath of this incident is somewhat surprising, given the significant evidence suggesting how much safer the technology tends to be in comparison to human-driven vehicles. Indeed, it appears more accurately to reflect the inherent dangers associated with human drivers rather than the failings of autonomous systems.

A single tragic death at the hands of an automated Uber halted its vehicle testing, yet it is crucial to recognize that approximately eighty-one percent of vehicular accidents are attributed to human error. This statistic starkly highlights the potential advantages of self-driving technology. Professionals working in urban areas like Oakland frequently observe that human error extends beyond just drivers; it encompasses pedestrians and cyclists as well. Humans often lack the necessary reaction time to adequately respond to unexpected changes in their environment, whether that involves a pedestrian stepping suddenly onto Forbes Avenue or another vehicle swerving abruptly to avoid a pothole. Moreover, human drivers are increasingly distracted, engaging in a dizzying array of activities that range from personal grooming to streaming television shows while behind the wheel. Self-driving cars could significantly mitigate these issues by providing a consistent, reliable response to road conditions and potential hazards. Additionally, autonomous vehicles address the pervasive problem of drunk driving, effectively serving as designated drivers. In a city like Pittsburgh, where taxi services are limited and commercial designated driving companies are virtually non-existent, the temptation for intoxicated individuals to drive from the vibrant Southside to the more suburban South Hills can have devastating consequences.

Accessibility and inclusion are also pressing concerns for Pittsburgh residents, particularly as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system designed to enhance current connections. However, there are significant worries that this initiative may overlook lower-income individuals and those with disabilities. Fleets of self-driving cars could provide crucial transportation options for those lacking access to personal vehicles, particularly in neighborhoods that are underserved by public transportation. The elderly and disabled populations in Pittsburgh, who previously benefitted from paratransit services that were unfortunately decimated by budget cuts, are especially vulnerable due to their limited mobility options. Autonomous vehicles could provide a lifeline for these individuals, offering them newfound independence and the ability to travel without relying on others.

Nevertheless, a counterargument to the accessibility claim is the high cost associated with self-driving vehicles. With an initial price tag often exceeding $100,000, there is little overlap between the demographic that currently relies on public transportation and those who can afford to purchase self-driving vehicles outright. Indeed, the financial burden of such high costs poses a significant barrier not just for public transit users, but for many potential car owners as well. However, this challenge may be alleviated by two key considerations. First, the cost of this technology is likely to decrease over time, following the historical trajectory of other technological advancements such as brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers, which once seemed prohibitively expensive but have since become accessible to a broader audience. What is unaffordable today may become attainable in the near future. Second, it remains uncertain whether Pittsburgh can shift its entrenched car-first mentality to embrace a more innovative public personal transit system that incorporates shared self-driving vehicles. Such a transition would require a cultural and infrastructural transformation, one that acknowledges the potential of autonomous technology to create a more inclusive and accessible transportation landscape for all residents.

It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are ridiculously simple! Compare to the history of bicycles for example. If you happen to have seen those old black and white photographs of early bicycles, you will see a complex evolution, akin to those in biology! Nevertheless, something on the horizon is going to turn everything about cars upside down (well, the cars themselves will probably still remain on their four wheels, though!)

Self-driving cars are already among us fueling the debate over their good and and evil. Proponents of automated cars argue that self-driving cars will eliminate the catastrophic number of fatal car crashes because the cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. The other feature they cite is the possibility for many, or even all, cars in a certain city to communicate with one another to coordinate the speed levels, and red light waits so as to achieve the maximum flow and minimum travel times. Automated cars, they say, will allow for more cars to drive next to each other, due to their precision in maneuvering, and yet lead to less congestion. Another major advantage will be their service to the disabled and elderly people. Once a good proportion of cars on the street become self-drivers, the advocates claim that the whole society will benefit: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from car accidents, less fuel consumption, fewer traffic police officers on duty, etc. 

However, the opponents of the technology have some legitimate concerns. In this category there are some superficial and trivial arguments that can be refuted right away. For example, the phobia of a scenario where a car breaks down and the human driver, having forgotten how to drive, has no idea of what to do, leading to chaos in the city cannot be a real argument against self-driving cars. Already today, an average individual has no skill to get a car to start up if it all of a sudden stops in the middle of street. Such a situation rarely disrupts a whole city! Some other arguments question the limits of the technology. What if it snows? What if the road is full of potholes (such as those in Pittsburgh!)? Well, these while valid concerns do not prove the technology insufficient. There are improvements coming every day and all those conditions will be taken care of.

With the obvious criticism of the self-driving cars out of way, we arrive at some deep issues against the technology. On the infrastructure level, there is the problem of providing cyber security to such a delicate network of interconnected software that store and communicate data between cars. If, as Guardian suggests, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, then it will be a true disaster. Achieving a high level of security while possible could be prohibitively costly, overshadowing any amount of saving on gasoline.

However, the biggest challenge facing self-driving cars is their popularity. The pros listed above all are predicated on the hope that they will come in large quantities. The effect of having cars coordinate their paths will not be felt if they substitute only 2% of all the cars out there. Same holds true for safety. Have you noticed that sometimes, ironically, the safest drivers, are the very cause of some accidents? Imagine a road where the speed limit is 50, but "everyone knows that it is ok to go 60 (when the police is not around!)". Then that one guy who sticks to that 50 mph limit is the source of frustration for those behind him/her and a possible danger for everyone involved. Thus, the advantages of autonomous cars will begin to take effect only after a good portion of the society adopts them.

But with the currently high cost of these cars, and the natural public resistance to change, it seems that self-driving cars will, for the coming years, live in the margins, only triggering imagination and debates from time to time. At this point in time, we are just not ready yet to embrace them -- definitely not in large numbers.

	Proponents of automated cars argue that self-driving cars will eliminate the stunning number of fatal car crashes, because the cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. 	As utopian as the technology seems, I have to side with the critics of the technology. There are several serious issues with the predictions above. 
	Firstly, self-driving cars will not necessarily be economical. Therefore, it is not clear whether self-driving cars will in fact be cost-efficient.

In case this optimistic assumption is not realized, the benefits promised by the technology will not be delivered. Self-driving cars can reduce the number of accidents only if they make up a significant percentage of all traffic. Other reasons may include people's natural phobia of drastic changes. We do hear people not willing to "give up the control" and "joy of driving". Such personal associations with cars and ethical issues (such as cars being programmed to minimize fatalities in an eventful situation by "directing the danger toward its own passengers") may turn out to be strong concerns for people that come in te way of transition to a driverless traffic.
	In summary, despite my praise for the engineering accomplishment, I do not believe that the society is ready for this revolution just yet. We may have to tolerate the boring junkers for a couple more decades.

It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are ridiculously simple! Compare this to the history of bicycles, for example. If you have seen those old black-and-white photographs of early bicycles, you will witness a complex evolution, akin to those found in biology. Nevertheless, something on the horizon is going to turn everything about cars upside down (well, the cars themselves will probably still remain on their four wheels, though!).

Self-driving cars are already among us, fueling a heated debate over their potential benefits and drawbacks. Proponents of automated vehicles argue convincingly that self-driving cars will significantly reduce the catastrophic number of fatal car crashes. They contend that these cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, pedestrians, or the influence of alcohol and drugs, which are common human failings. Additionally, advocates point to the possibility of cars within a certain city communicating with one another to coordinate their speed and synchronize traffic lights, ultimately achieving maximum flow and minimizing travel times. This technological advancement could lead to more cars driving in close proximity due to their precision in maneuvering, thereby reducing congestion on busy roads. Another major advantage touted by supporters is the potential for self-driving cars to serve the disabled and elderly populations, granting them newfound mobility and independence. Once a substantial proportion of vehicles on the streets become self-driving, advocates claim that society as a whole will benefit through saved lives, reduced financial burdens from accidents, less fuel consumption, and a decreased need for traffic police officers.

However, opponents of this technology raise some legitimate concerns that warrant serious consideration. While some arguments against self-driving cars may seem superficial or trivial—such as the fear of a human driver losing their skills to control a vehicle in the event of a breakdown—their validity cannot be dismissed entirely. The reality is that even today, many individuals are ill-equipped to handle a car that unexpectedly stalls in the middle of the street, which rarely causes widespread chaos. More substantive concerns focus on the limitations of the technology itself. For instance, questions arise about how self-driving cars will perform in adverse conditions, such as heavy snowfall or on poorly maintained roads, like those riddled with potholes in cities like Pittsburgh. While these are valid concerns, they do not definitively prove the inadequacy of self-driving technology. Innovations are continually emerging, and it is likely that many of these challenges will be addressed through ongoing advancements.

Once we move past the more obvious criticisms of self-driving cars, we encounter deeper issues related to the technology. At the infrastructure level, a significant challenge lies in ensuring cybersecurity for the interconnected network of software that stores and communicates data between vehicles. If, as reported by The Guardian, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, the consequences could be catastrophic. Achieving a high level of security is feasible but could incur prohibitive costs, potentially outweighing any savings garnered from reduced gasoline consumption.

However, the most substantial challenge facing self-driving cars is their acceptance among the public. The advantages listed above hinge on the assumption that these vehicles will be widely adopted. The benefits of coordinated driving systems will remain negligible if only a small percentage—say, 2%—of vehicles on the road are autonomous. This scenario parallels the phenomenon of safe drivers inadvertently causing accidents; consider a road where the speed limit is 50 mph, but "everyone knows" that it is acceptable to drive at 60 mph when the police are absent. In such cases, the individual who adheres strictly to the speed limit could become a source of frustration for other drivers and potentially create dangerous situations. Therefore, the full advantages of autonomous cars will only materialize when a significant portion of society embraces them.

Yet, with the current high cost of self-driving vehicles, combined with the public's natural resistance to change, it appears that self-driving cars will remain on the fringes of society for the foreseeable future. They will likely continue to spark imagination and debate but will not see mass adoption in the immediate term. At this point in time, we are simply not ready to fully embrace self-driving technology—certainly not in large numbers. My stance is clear: while self-driving cars offer promising benefits, the hurdles they face suggest that we will need to tread carefully as we navigate towards an autonomous future.

This includes handicapped people and elderly-- those unable to drive for physically, mentally, or emotionally debilitating reasons. 
	Another promising motivator  is that artificial intelligence can detect risky driving situations. Computers are alert--technically 100% of the time--to possible perils, such as objects which might be entering the driver's immediate field. The passengers in car with an omnipotent driver  will not need to worry about emergency situations. For example, autonomous braking will occur automatically  because the computer is programmed to follow a paradigm to bring the car to an immediate stop. The thought of these abrupt movement and how they might affect passengers is a bit frightening to me. Can the passengers be injured inside the vehicle due to sudden movements? This would be part of the further investigation that I want to see before I place my full confidence in self-driving vehicles. 
	The idea that the computer/driver can control speed is new, though the technology of cruise control has been around for years, though an elected option. Since high speed is a cause of accidents, this would be an advantage to self-driving cars, not allowing the driver the option to speed.
Perhaps the most advantageous aspect of the self-driving car--and the most tempting in regard to lulling me to accept them-- is the apparent ease of parking the car. I don't seem to have a ""knack"" for parking my car as so many other drivers seem to have. 
	And considering that in today's world, parking spaces are becoming  fewer and smaller, and with less room all the time to negotiate parking that car, the self-driving car comes to the rescue. My feelings of nervousness will be relieved if the self-driving car can take the wheel and perform the conglomeration of skills and negotiations for me. 
	Though swayed by parking, I must attest that I am  not at all comfortable with submitting the full power of driving my car to a computer. I've witnessed the malfunction of computers, and this occurrence would put not only the driver at risk, but other occupants of the vehicle as well as unsuspecting pedestrians. . 
	The most frightening thought to me is a malfunctioning computer that can create a more dangerous  situation than if the driver were manning the vehicle. My distrust stems from an experience I remember from my youth. In the blockbuster movie, 2001, A Space Odyssey, computers conducted all of the affairs of the spaceship. The human commander of the spacecraft began to suspect that  the computer, named Hal, was malfunctioning and would need to be shut down or put out of commission. Unfortunately, even though  the commander was careful to not allow the computer to be within earshot of the conversations he'd had with a member of his crew, the computer was able to ""see"" the 2 men speaking. Hal was able to read their lips, thus being tipped to the nature of their distrust. Then Hal began plotting ways to get rid of the men to save "his" own life, so that he could not be dismantled. 
	While such robotic intricacies may not be a factor in determining the safety of such cars, there is also the act of terrorism spawned by the human element which can wreak havoc if the computer system was  hacked. So because of these lingering concerns, I cannot at this time place my full confidence in the self-driving car.
