However, self driving cars are the way of the future. The pros of having self driving cars on the road vastly outweigh the cons. The effects of self driving cars will benefit society in a multitude of ways, and will allow mankind to advance further in technology and science alike.

There are many ways that the advent of self driving cars will be beneficial to mankind. For example, many people get distracted while driving, which is one of the leading causes of motor vehicle accidents in the United States as of now. However, if a computer were to take control of the car, there would no distraction; the computer would stay completely focused on the road, effectively cutting the amount of accidents greatly. This has more implications than just saving human life, there would also be much more money saved as a result of this. Right now there is 9.2 million dollars saved for accidents on the road, less accidents would mean less of this money would have to be saved and could be used in other areas such as infrastructure. Additionally, many cars even have features that resemble early stages of self driving cars; most cars have sensors to detect objects, have autonomous breaking, and many other driver-less car like features. Making the step to fully autonomous cars is not actually as big as a jump as critics may believe. There are many benefits that self driving cars may have for society, and this op-ed only covers a fraction of how self driving cars have the potential to help humanity.

Even with all of the benefits that self driving cars have to offer, there are still some things that critics are worried about. For example, many people worry that people may not have the skills needed to operate cars, and if there was a glitch, this may turn into a serious issue. However, there could easily be regulations enacted that would require drivers to take a test every year or so, to ensure that they can take over in the case of an emergency. Another common concern is handing over the power of the car to a computer, which can malfunction with a small coding error. This is not a valid concern though, as each car would be thoroughly tested before consumer use, and furthermore, humans have even more capacity to 'malfunction' and cause an accident that computers do! Though people may have concerns about the effects of self driving cars on society, they will ultimately accomplish more good than they will harm.

Self driving cars is the subject of much scrutiny. There are many people with strong opinions for both sides. However, after reading this op-ed, I hope that I have convinced you of all of the benefits that self driving cars can bring, and I hope that you are now a supporter of the idea.

For example, people can get easily distracted while driving, which is one of the leading causes of motor vehicle accidents in the United States as of today. However it is not possible for a computer to become distracted, it does not have personality or curiosity like humans do; thus, if computers controlled the wheel, accidents on the road would decline greatly. This has more implications than just saving human life, there would be a significant fiscal advantage as well. Right now there is 9.2 million dollars saved for motor vehicle accidents, however if the roads became safer, then less of this money would need to be allocated for accidents and could be used in other areas. Additionally, many cars even have features that resemble early stages of self driving cars; most cars have object sensors, autonomous breaking, and many other driver-less car like features. Consequently, the step to fully autonomous cars is not actually as big as some critics may believe. As seen by these few examples, self driving cars have immense potential to help society, and therefore we should be more open to release them in public. 
	Even with all of the benefits that self driving cars have to offer, there are still some critiques. For example, people worry that passengers may not have the skills needed to operate cars (why would you learn how to drive a car if it drove itself?), and if there was a glitch, it may turn into a serious issue. However, there could easily be regulations enacted that would require drivers to take a test every year or so, to ensure that they can take over in the case of an emergency. Just as drivers have to prove they can drive a car today, they would have to do the same in the future. Another common concern is the cars driving ability in hazardous weather conditions. Some sensors can be notably damaged by heavy rain and wind. This concern is not as serious as it may seem however. If there was regulation passed to make sure humans still knew how to drive, than they could just take over during inclement weather. Furthermore, if the weather conditions were that dangerous, than it would not be advisable to go driving anyway! Though people may have concerns about the effects of self driving cars on society, they will ultimately accomplish more good than they will harm.
I imagine a future where kids can play outside safely, without worrying about a ball rolling onto a busy street, a world where parents did not have to fear that their newly initiated teenage driver would make it to his friend in one piece, and a place that has enough money to upkeep public transportation and roads. This world that I imagine, and that I hope you imagine too, can come as a direct followup from self driving cars. Self driving cars are not perfect yet, and there are still many issues to be worked out. Despite this, I believe that with all the potential benefits that self driving cars can bring to society, they should be not only adopted, but embraced. They can help with many different aspects of society, but only if we let them.

According to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), distracted driving was responsible for nearly 9,500 fatalities in 2019 alone. In contrast, computers, equipped with advanced sensors and algorithms, can maintain focus on the road without succumbing to distractions. This shift from human drivers to automated systems could dramatically reduce the incidence of accidents, saving thousands of lives each year. By minimizing the number of accidents, self-driving cars could alleviate this financial burden, allowing funds to be redirected to vital infrastructure projects, healthcare, and education.

Moreover, many vehicles today incorporate features that are precursors to full automation. Many already have sensors to detect obstacles, lane-keeping assistance, and autonomous braking systems. The transition to fully autonomous vehicles is not as monumental as critics might suggest; instead, it is a natural progression in automotive technology. However, regulations could be established to require drivers to undergo periodic testing to ensure they can regain control in emergencies. This proposal would not only maintain driving skills but also instill a sense of responsibility among users of autonomous vehicles. Another prevalent concern is the idea of relinquishing control to a computer, which could be prone to glitches or errors. However, it is essential to consider that the technology behind self-driving cars is continually evolving, with rigorous testing protocols in place. The industry has learned from past technological failures and has implemented robust safety measures to mitigate risks. Furthermore, human drivers are not infallible; studies indicate that human error accounts for approximately 94% of car crashes, demonstrating that computers may actually reduce the risk of accidents compared to human drivers.

In conclusion, self-driving cars are a subject of much scrutiny and debate. There are strong opinions on both sides of the issue. By embracing this technology, we can enhance safety, improve economic efficiency, and pave the way for a more innovative future. I encourage you to join the conversation and support the advancement of self-driving technology, as it holds the promise of transforming our roads for the better.

To say that society should stifle its growth in the fears of setting legal precedence on a new issue is to prevent the discovery of solutions to current and future issues. output_text:
Self-driving cars seem to be a thing of the future, but as reports of self-driving cars on the streets keep coming up in the media, their dawn on society is evident. There are several proponents against the use of self-driving cars, citing issues of increased fatalities, higher costs, and unresolved legal claims. Nevertheless, manufacturers continue to push for testing of this technology, and the positives of self-driving cars far outweigh the negatives.

One of the most significant arguments against the implementation of self-driving cars is the fear of an increased likelihood of accidents. Critics argue that with no human at the wheel, a computer is more prone to making mistakes that could result in accidents or even fatalities. While this perspective raises valid concerns, it often focuses on the most sensational outcomes without considering the broader context. In fact, studies show that approximately 81 percent of car crashes are caused by human error, highlighting a fundamental issue in road safety. The introduction of self-driving cars, equipped with advanced sensors and algorithms, has the potential to eliminate this percentage of accidents entirely. Furthermore, it is essential to recognize that while there have been isolated incidents involving self-driving cars, the overall rate of accidents attributed to these vehicles is likely to be significantly lower than those caused by human drivers. The technology is designed to learn from a vast array of driving scenarios, enabling it to react more effectively to potential hazards than a human driver might.

Another common argument against self-driving cars is the perceived high cost of implementing the necessary technology. Admittedly, the initial investment in computer software, sensors, and extensive research and development can be substantial. However, this perspective often overlooks the long-term financial benefits associated with self-driving vehicles. The U.S. Department of Transportation estimates that the value of a human life is around $9.2 million, a figure that insurance companies also use when evaluating risk. By shifting the responsibility of driving from humans to machines, the potential for fatal accidents decreases, which could lead to significant reductions in insurance premiums and healthcare costs associated with car accidents. Additionally, the time savings provided by self-driving cars could translate into economic benefits. If individuals no longer need to focus on driving, they can utilize that time for productive activities, whether for work or personal enrichment. This newfound efficiency could lead to an increase in economic output, thereby justifying the initial costs of the technology.

A final argument presented against self-driving cars is the lack of established legal precedents to address accidents caused by these vehicles. Critics express concerns about the complexities of determining liability in the event of an accident involving a self-driving car. Indeed, as we navigate the uncharted waters of this technology, questions of responsibility and accountability will undoubtedly arise. However, stifling technological advancement due to fears of legal ambiguity would hinder progress and prevent society from discovering solutions to evolving challenges. It is crucial that we embrace these innovations and work collaboratively to establish a legal framework that addresses the unique circumstances presented by self-driving technology. If self-driving cars can serve as a solution for a safer, more efficient, and cost-effective future, it is our responsibility to ensure that this technology is allowed to flourish.

In conclusion, while there are valid concerns regarding self-driving cars, the potential benefits significantly outweigh the drawbacks. By addressing issues of safety, cost, and legal liability, society can harness the advantages of this transformative technology, paving the way for a future where driving is safer and more efficient for everyone.

output_text:
Up with Self-Driving Cars and Down with Gasoline Industry

     Self-driving cars will eliminate many accidents and significantly reduce the detrimental impact of the gasoline industry, which contributes to excessive pollution and whose relevance is quickly fading. The computers that power self-driving cars will not be distracted like human drivers. Research shows that the average attention span is only eight seconds, which means a person might not react quickly enough to avoid a deer in the road or a bicyclist passing by. Alarmingly, human error accounts for approximately 94% of traffic accidents, with a significant portion of these incidents stemming from drunk and distracted driving.

     Self-driving cars have the potential to dramatically decrease the number of accidents, particularly those caused by distracted drivers. Distracted driving is a pervasive issue that can take many forms: people often engage in texting or talking on their cell phones, eating and drinking, conversing with passengers, or fiddling with the radio. Additionally, distractions such as children and pets in the vehicle, or even grooming activities like applying makeup, can divert attention from the road. By employing sophisticated sensors and advanced algorithms, self-driving cars can navigate safely, minimizing the risk associated with these human distractions.

     Moreover, self-driving technology aims to eliminate other common causes of accidents, including speeding, impaired or reckless driving, running red lights and stop signs, unsafe lane changes, wrong-way driving, road rage, and drowsy driving. These vehicles are equipped with the ability to monitor their surroundings continuously and react instantaneously to potential hazards, thereby creating a safer driving environment for everyone on the road.

     In addition to enhancing safety, self-driving cars will likely be electric, which represents a significant step forward for environmental sustainability. Electric vehicles (EVs) are inherently more efficient than their gasoline-powered counterparts and produce considerably less pollution. The operation of electric cars is not only quieter but also smoother, contrasting sharply with the clunky and outdated mechanics of gas-operated vehicles. From a financial perspective, electric cars offer substantial savings; they typically cost about one-third to one-quarter of the price of gasoline to operate, and their maintenance costs are lower since they do not require oil changes. Furthermore, the absence of a tailpipe in electric vehicles means that they contribute to improved air quality, which is essential for public health.

     The gasoline industry, on the other hand, is a major contributor to air pollution and environmental degradation. The harmful emissions from gas-powered cars, including benzene and other carcinogenic chemicals, pose serious health risks. Prolonged exposure to gasoline fumes can lead to a range of health issues, such as confusion, dizziness, headaches, and respiratory difficulties. Given that fossil fuels are finite resources and their extraction has significant environmental consequences, it is clear that transitioning to electric vehicles is not just beneficial; it is imperative for the health of our planet and its inhabitants.

     Transitioning from gasoline vehicles to electric ones does not necessitate job losses; rather, it presents an opportunity for upskilling and adaptation. Mechanics currently working in gas garages can be trained to service electric vehicles, or they can shift to other roles within the evolving transportation landscape. Historically, when society moved from horse and buggy to automobiles, the focus was not on the livelihoods of those who raised horses; instead, progress was embraced for the greater good. Likewise, we must not allow concerns about job displacement to hinder the inevitable progress toward safer and more sustainable transportation solutions. As public transportation options like buses and ride-sharing services continue to thrive, individuals who do not own vehicles will still have employment opportunities, ensuring a balanced transition into a new era of mobility.

In conclusion, the shift toward self-driving and electric cars represents a transformative change in our approach to transportation. By embracing these technologies, we can create a safer, cleaner, and more efficient future. The time has come to champion self-driving cars and support the decline of the gasoline industry for the benefit of society and the environment.

draft1:
Self driving cars are a highly debated topic simply because they pose virtually equal amounts of advantages and disadvantages. They offer more efficient transportation, mobility for all people, theoretically safer roads, and overall convenience by improving quality of life. In contrast, they also threaten jobs for those who work in transportation, pose danger in the event of a malfunction, are unable to understand human intention, and rely on somewhat faulty GPS systems. With such important advantages and disadvantages one has to ask if the risk is worth it. Society does not really need self driving cars, but given the direction of technological advances of late, self driving cars seem like a logical next step. The whole point of the digital age essentially is to improve life for everyone and self driving cars have enough potential to do that. Self driving cars are beneficial to society because their potential benefits outweigh the solvable costs they pose. 

The benefits of self driving cars are so wonderful that they diminish the argument to be made against self driving cars. It's no secret that humans are consumed by technology and appear to not get enough of it. The evidence lies in the fact that we can not seem to stay focused on the road for the duration of a car ride without checking our phones for insignificant messages and calls. These seemingly innocent phone checks while driving end up claiming the lives of thousands per year despite laws against texting or calling while on the road. Take Maryland for example, a state with some of the strictest laws against using a cell phone while driving yet it still ranks as one of the highest states for the worst drivers and most fatal accidents. People will continue to break the law as they please, but a computer will not. The computer in a self driving car can not be distracted as shown in pro number 3. For those that argue that yes a computer can not get distracted but they can malfunction I offer this rebuttal: Cars malfunction all the time even without a computer in them. I've read and heard numerous news stories about people driving and all of a sudden their breaks do not work causing them to crash into other drivers. In addition, malfunctions can be easily prevented if not at least securely managed if car companies are forced to offer routine check ups and software updates for the self driving cars. Essentially all of the cons about self driving cars are solvable and preventable problems that fail to overcast a permanent reluctance for their existence. Take for example the argument that self driving cars can not interpret human traffic signals. Embed a software that allows the cars to recognize when a light is out and a human is directing traffic. The car can analyze the human or ask the human in the car what it should do. Of course this is no easy feat but there are enough intelligent people in the world to figure this issue out and others just like it. From being better for the environment to reducing traffic and increasing mobility for the elderly and disabled, self driving cars have countless benefits that society seems to be passing up on.

Self driving cars present more benefits than costs, therefore, they should be implemented in the technology of tomorrow. If more information is shared about self driving cars and how their potential costs can be managed and prevented more people will be willing to make the switch and live a safer, easier life. The decision is simple, the technology exists, the only real debate here is whether society is ready to finally start living in the "future".

output_text:
Self-driving cars are a highly debated topic, primarily because they present virtually equal amounts of advantages and disadvantages. On one hand, they promise more efficient transportation, increased mobility for all individuals—including the elderly and disabled—safer roads through advanced technology, and overall convenience that could significantly enhance quality of life. On the other hand, they threaten jobs for those employed in the transportation sector, pose risks in the event of a malfunction, struggle to interpret human intentions, and rely on GPS systems that can be unreliable. With such significant advantages and disadvantages, one must ponder whether the risks are worth taking. While society may not fundamentally require self-driving cars, the trajectory of technological advancements suggests that they are a logical next step. The essence of the digital age is to improve life for everyone, and self-driving cars possess sufficient potential to achieve that goal. Therefore, I argue that self-driving cars are beneficial to society because their potential benefits outweigh the solvable challenges they present.

The advantages of self-driving cars are compelling enough to overshadow many of the arguments against their implementation. It is no secret that humans are increasingly consumed by technology, often unable to maintain focus on the road for the duration of a car ride without succumbing to the temptation of checking phones for trivial messages and calls. This seemingly innocuous behavior has dire consequences, claiming thousands of lives annually, despite stringent laws prohibiting texting or calling while driving. For instance, Maryland, a state known for its strict regulations on cell phone use behind the wheel, ranks among the highest in terms of poor driving and fatal accidents. This illustrates a fundamental truth: while people may choose to disregard the law, a computer system in a self-driving car will not be distracted. Unlike human drivers, the sophisticated algorithms and sensors that govern self-driving vehicles are designed to maintain focus and react to their environment without succumbing to distractions or emotional responses.

Critics may argue that while computers are not easily distracted, they are susceptible to malfunctions. However, it is essential to recognize that car malfunctions have been a reality long before the advent of self-driving technology. Numerous news stories recount instances of mechanical failures—such as brake failures—that lead to catastrophic accidents. These issues, while serious, can be mitigated through rigorous safety protocols, mandatory routine check-ups, and software updates imposed on manufacturers of self-driving cars. The notion that malfunctions could be fatal should not deter society from pursuing this technology; rather, it highlights the need for stringent maintenance and oversight.

Moreover, concerns regarding self-driving cars' inability to interpret human traffic signals can be addressed through the integration of advanced software. For instance, systems can be developed that enable self-driving cars to recognize when a traffic light is out and a human is directing traffic, allowing the vehicle to analyze the situation or even consult a passenger for guidance. While such innovations present technical challenges, they are not insurmountable. The world is filled with intelligent minds capable of solving these problems, as the field of artificial intelligence continues to advance rapidly.

In addition to enhancing safety, self-driving cars offer numerous societal benefits, such as environmental sustainability, reduced traffic congestion, and improved mobility for marginalized groups. By minimizing fuel consumption through optimized driving patterns, self-driving cars could also contribute to reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Furthermore, the elderly and individuals with disabilities would gain unprecedented independence, allowing them to travel without relying on others for transportation.

In conclusion, self-driving cars present more benefits than costs, thereby warranting their integration into the technological landscape of tomorrow. As information about their potential advantages and manageable costs becomes more widely disseminated, public acceptance is likely to grow. The decision to adopt this technology is straightforward; the technology is available, and the primary debate revolves around society's readiness to embrace the future. With a commitment to addressing and solving the challenges presented by self-driving cars, we can pave the way for a safer, more efficient, and more inclusive transportation system.

output_text:
Self-driving cars are an adaptation of the auto-pilot mode used in airplanes, representing a technological breakthrough with the potential to reshape urban transportation. This innovation holds particular promise for people living in densely populated cities, where pollution levels are high, traffic congestion is rampant, and available space for vehicles is dwindling. Imagine being able to sit comfortably in a climate-controlled vehicle, perhaps catching up on sleep for half an hour, and arriving at your destination without the stress of navigating through traffic or searching for parking. 

However, like every coin has two sides, this new technology presents both advantages and disadvantages. On one hand, self-driving cars can alleviate concerns about inclement weather, allowing passengers to travel safely without worrying about rain or snow affecting their journey. On the other hand, there are legitimate concerns regarding the reliability of the car's sensors and systems; a malfunction could lead to catastrophic loss of control. 

Additionally, the implications for insurance coverage are complex. With autonomous vehicles making the majority of driving decisions, the traditional model of liability insurance could be challenged. If a self-driving car is involved in an accident, determining who is at fault becomes problematic—there may be no clear party to blame, complicating liability and compensation processes.

Despite these challenges, the potential applications of self-driving technology are vast. It can streamline emergency healthcare responses, optimize traffic management to reduce congestion, and facilitate closer parking options for users. However, it is crucial to recognize that this technology is still in its developmental phase, and the stakes are incredibly high. The environment in which these vehicles operate is unpredictable. There could be a drunk driver veering into the lane, a child unexpectedly darting into the street, or adverse weather conditions creating hazardous driving scenarios. These are all factors that autonomous vehicles must navigate, and the inability to control such variables raises significant safety concerns.

After evaluating the pros and cons with careful consideration, I firmly believe that self-driving cars represent the future of transportation. A compelling argument in favor of this technology is its successful application in aviation and systems like Automatic People Movers at airports, showcasing that autonomous vehicles can operate effectively in controlled environments.

Support for self-driving cars is gaining traction among major technology companies, which view this innovation as a transformative opportunity. Moreover, self-driving technology can significantly enhance the quality of life for individuals with disabilities, granting them independence and ease of access to education and employment opportunities. No longer will they have to rely on public transportation, which can be insufficient or inaccessible.

The beauty of self-driving cars lies in their user-friendliness; they do not require technical knowledge to operate. Individuals of all ages, from young children to elderly adults, can travel independently without the need for a companion. In bustling cities like New York, Shanghai, Hong Kong, and Mumbai, where residents spend countless hours stuck in traffic, the implementation of self-driving technology could lead to considerable time savings and reductions in vehicular pollution.

All these factors present a compelling case for embracing this mode of transportation. If there remains any doubt about the benefits of self-driving cars, consider these scenarios: a drunk driver on the road, a pregnant woman urgently needing to reach the hospital, a mother managing two crying children in the back seat, or an elderly person struggling to cope with the glare of oncoming headlights. If self-driving cars can safely transport these individuals to their destinations, it becomes clear how transformative and beneficial this technology can be for society as a whole.

output_text:
Society is constantly growing and adapting in regards to its technology. Products becoming available to the public nowadays would have been completely unfathomable years prior. One futuristic concept currently being tested is the idea of self-driving cars. This technology is appealing to society for numerous reasons: increased accessibility for individuals with disabilities, a perceived reduction in accidents, and predicted improvements in traffic efficiency, among others. However, there are also significant drawbacks to the implementation of self-driving cars within society, including high costs, potential program malfunctions, a future decline in driving competency, and a potential loss of jobs for those currently employed in driving-related fields. Despite the potential benefits of self-driving cars, ultimately, the cons outweigh the advantages they could offer to society.

According to the article, one of the primary motivations behind the desire for self-driving cars is the belief that they will substantially reduce accidents on the roads. However, numerous factors must align perfectly for this to be realized. Passengers still need to possess a fundamental understanding of how to operate the vehicle in order to intervene in emergencies and avoid accidents; human error remains a potential risk, revealing a critical flaw in the assumption of accident-free travel. Additionally, adverse weather conditions can complicate the functioning of the car's laser sensors, which could impair the vehicle's ability to navigate safely. In such instances, it becomes imperative for the passenger to be prepared to take over control, thereby introducing the possibility of human error contributing to accidents. Moreover, self-driving cars cannot be programmed with every conceivable local driving law, potential road hazard, or construction rerouting, creating scenarios where the vehicles may struggle to respond appropriately. Given that GPS systems are not always reliable, the ability of a human driver to make real-time corrections becomes invaluable. If self-driving cars lack accurate information, the likelihood of accidents increases significantly. There is simply no way to guarantee that self-driving cars can drastically reduce accidents, and the legal ramifications of accidents involving autonomous vehicles further complicate the issue. In the event of an accident, questions will arise regarding liability: is it the car's fault, the manufacturer's, or the passenger's?

Self-driving cars also pose more challenges than benefits to society as a whole. For one, the majority of the general public may find these vehicles financially out of reach. Current estimates place the costs for engineering, power, and other associated expenses at a minimum of $100,000 per vehicle. To truly realize the benefits of self-driving technology, it must be widely adopted within society; however, this widespread implementation seems impractical given the high costs. Furthermore, the introduction of self-driving cars threatens to displace numerous jobs that are integral to the functioning of our economy. Professions such as auto manufacturing, driver's education, personal injury law, and the gasoline industry could all experience significant job losses due to decreased demand for human drivers. This could lead to rising unemployment rates and negatively impact the overall economy, as many individuals would be left struggling to find new employment opportunities in a rapidly changing job market.

While technology offers remarkable advantages in modern society, it should only be applied where it is genuinely needed. Would we want to inhabit a world where the population has lost the essential ability to operate a vehicle? A society so reliant on technology that a simple glitch or recall could leave them stranded without any means of transportation? An innovation that inadvertently leads to the destruction of countless jobs for hardworking Americans? The potential dilemmas associated with self-driving cars—economic, moral, and safety-related—are significant. Why navigate these complex issues when the task of manually driving a car could effectively mitigate many of these concerns? In conclusion, while self-driving cars may present an appealing vision of the future, the reality is that their implications could be far more detrimental than beneficial for society as a whole.

output_text:
Technology is evolving every day and has become an integral part of our lives as humans living in the 21st century. It feels almost like living in a science fiction movie. From our phones and TVs to our refrigerators, everything seems to be 'smart.' Soon, we might even be able to add our cars to that list. A world with self-driving cars may not be too far off, as companies like Google have been rigorously testing vehicles that can navigate on their own in various states. However, the question arises: are these self-driving cars truly more beneficial for society? While some may argue that self-driving cars are advantageous because they are safer than human drivers and could significantly reduce the number of car accidents, I contend that they would not be more beneficial overall. The costs associated with self-driving cars are substantial—not only in terms of money but potentially in the loss of lives as well.

Statistics indicate that upwards of 33,000 people die annually in car accidents, leading many to believe that self-driving cars could serve as a viable solution to this critical issue. Proponents of this technology often highlight that car accidents are predominantly caused by human error, such as distractions or impaired judgment. They argue that self-driving cars, operated by sophisticated computer systems, would be immune to such distractions, thereby drastically lowering the probability of fatal accidents. These vehicles utilize complex algorithms that enable them to be acutely aware of their surroundings and respond more quickly than human drivers, which could be instrumental in preventing collisions. Moreover, given that the value of a human life is estimated to be around $9.2 million, the reduction in accidents could theoretically lead to significant financial savings for society as a whole.

However, despite these seemingly positive attributes, self-driving cars could ultimately do more harm than good. Firstly, the costs associated with these vehicles are likely to be much higher than anticipated. While accidents due to human error may decrease, the risk of accidents arising from computer malfunctions remains a significant concern. These errors could still result in tragic loss of life, perpetuating the cycle of grief and financial burden. Additionally, in the event of an accident involving a self-driving car, the question of liability becomes murky. Who should be held accountable—the manufacturer, the software developer, or the vehicle owner? Such legal disputes could lead to lengthy and costly battles, creating further complications for the victims and their families.

Moreover, the widespread adoption of self-driving cars poses a serious threat to numerous jobs within the transportation sector. Professions such as taxi drivers, delivery personnel, and truck drivers could face significant unemployment as automation takes over. This shift would not only impact the livelihoods of these individuals but also disrupt local economies that rely on these jobs. Additionally, the insurance industry could experience a seismic shift, as the need for traditional car insurance may diminish, leading to job losses and economic instability in that sector as well.

Given our increasing reliance on technology, self-driving cars may initially appear to be a groundbreaking advancement. Yet, while they may improve certain aspects of human life, they could also severely impact the livelihoods of countless individuals and introduce new risks to public safety. Therefore, it is crucial to approach the implementation of self-driving cars with caution. The potential dangers and economic repercussions warrant a critical examination. For these reasons, I firmly believe we should reconsider the push for self-driving cars.

This can be very hard, especially in a location where public transportation is not developed and/or in busy cities where other people are always in hurry.

It also minimizes risks associated with the self-driving technologies in the future as we collect driving data, which would decrease the number of car accidents and therefore offer a better experience for people.

Technological advances have made our society more convenient and efficient in various areas, including social networking services, shopping experiences, and personalized online streaming services. However, there has always been controversy regarding whether technology is improving or deteriorating our society. One of the most significant advancements in recent years is the development of self-driving cars, which are currently being tested with sophisticated algorithms designed to enhance safety on the roads. Despite the potential for these technologies to revolutionize transportation, many people remain skeptical about their safety, often citing the risks of increased accidents. While I acknowledge that these risks can be substantial, I firmly believe that self-driving technology has the potential to create significant opportunities for individuals facing mobility challenges and can lead to continual improvements in road safety through data collection and analysis.

First, self-driving cars can be immensely beneficial not only for the general population but particularly for individuals with disabilities, pregnant women, and others who face challenges when it comes to driving. Many of these individuals rely heavily on public transportation or require assistance for their mobility needs, which can be particularly burdensome in areas where public transport is underdeveloped or in bustling urban centers where transportation options are limited. Self-driving cars offer a novel, personalized solution for those who cannot drive or struggle to utilize public transportation effectively. While there are legitimate concerns regarding the safety of self-driving vehicles—such as their performance in adverse weather conditions or complex traffic scenarios—it's essential to recognize that human drivers also pose significant risks, including distractions, intoxication, and fatigue. Given that there are inherent dangers in all forms of driving, it seems prudent to explore the introduction of self-driving cars as a means to enhance mobility for those who currently face obstacles in their daily lives.

Moreover, the risks associated with self-driving technology can be mitigated through the systematic collection and analysis of data. One of the primary advantages of self-driving cars is their capacity to record vast amounts of operational data. In the event of an accident, this data can be thoroughly analyzed to uncover the underlying causes, leading to improvements in the algorithms that govern these vehicles. This proactive approach to safety is in stark contrast to the challenges faced when accidents are attributed to human error. Identifying and rectifying the precise cause of an incident involving human drivers is often fraught with difficulties, and even when contributing factors are identified, implementing immediate changes can be slow and inefficient. While educational programs and policy changes exist to promote safer driving practices, these measures can take significant time to yield tangible results. In contrast, the data-driven nature of self-driving technology allows for rapid iterations and enhancements, which can directly contribute to reducing accidents over time.

In conclusion, I maintain that the introduction of self-driving cars holds the potential to provide new opportunities for individuals who struggle with driving and alternative means of transportation. The risks associated with self-driving technologies, while real, can be systematically minimized through rigorous data collection and analysis, ultimately leading to a decline in car accidents and a better overall transportation experience for everyone. Embracing this technology could represent a significant step forward in making our roads safer and more accessible for all members of society.

Since computers are not affected by these factors and instead use precise algorithms to drive, this greatly reduces the risk of accidents. Unfortunately, there are some disadvantages to self-driving cars. Computers are unable to respond to human signals and unpredictable situations, which may be a serious safety concern. The use of self-driving cars may also have a negative effect on the economy because it eliminates many jobs such as taxi drivers and driver’s education instructors, as well as jobs in the gasoline industry since self-driving cars are likely to be electric. However, the advantages in safety, efficiency, and accessibility of self-driving cars outweigh these possible risks and demonstrate that self-driving cars would be a very beneficial addition to our society. 
	Self-driving cars have many advantages when it comes to efficiency in cost and time. With fewer accidents on the road, insurance and healthcare costs will be reduced. Drivers can also save time and money by completing other tasks during driving time since they do not have to focus on the road. If self-driving cars become widespread, they can improve traffic and road safety. In large groups, self-driving cars participate in a practice called “platooning”. The cars are able to communicate with each other, which helps to reduce traffic and hazardous situations on the road. This saves time, gasoline, and also allows cars to travel at higher speeds. Overall, the efficiency of self-driving cars reduces traffic and makes the road a safer place. 
	Self-driving cars also give access to transportation to those who cannot drive cars themselves, such as disabled and elderly individuals. Disabled individuals often rely on others for transportation or have to use public transportation, greatly inhibiting their mobility. Access to self-driving cars would allow these individuals to travel in a more convenient way, granting them more freedom. This is true for elderly individuals as well. People often worry about allowing their elderly parents to drive at a certain age, concerned about their safety but not wanting to take away their independence. With self-driving cars, this would not be an issue since the cars drive themselves. 
	Despite these benefits, many are still skeptical of self-driving cars. Using new technology can be risky, since new challenges often arise. For example, self-driving cars have a greater chance of malfunction in certain weather conditions, and are also unable to follow human traffic signals such as a police officer directing traffic. As humans become more accustomed to self-driving cars, they may begin to lose the skills necessary to operate a vehicle. Becoming too reliant on technology may be an issue, as the car could malfunction, and the driver may be in a more dangerous situation than if they were operating a human driven car. While these concerns are certainly notable, the safety advantages of self-driving cars offset the possible risks. Research shows that 81 percent of car crashes are due to human error. Many factors can lead to unsafe driving for humans, such as distractions, driving under the influence of drugs or alcohol, and speeding. Since computers are not affected by these factors, the chance of an accident is greatly reduced. Computers are able to make calculations in order to stop a safe distance away from other objects, decreasing the probability of a crash. Self-driving cars will save many lives and should be considered in spite of the possible concerns. 
	As technology continues to advance, there are many new possibilities of improvement in transportation. Self-driving cars may sound like an invention from the future, but they will likely be on the roads sooner than expected. Though the technology for self-driving cars is not completed yet, these cars demonstrate how technology improves safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If their development is successful, the use of these cars will have a positive impact on the transportation industry and society in general.

In our rapidly developing society, new technology significantly impacts various industries, particularly transportation. As innovations continue to emerge, many vehicles are now equipped with advanced features such as motion sensors, lane-keeping assistance, and autonomous braking systems. With the increasing prevalence of these technologies, a pressing question arises: will self-driving cars eventually dominate our roads? Proponents argue that self-driving cars are inherently safer than human-driven vehicles due to the elimination of human error. Additionally, they have the potential to enhance efficiency, as computers can manage driving responsibilities more effectively than humans, and they offer greater accessibility to individuals who are unable to drive. However, there are notable disadvantages to self-driving cars, including the inability of computers to respond to human signals and unpredictable circumstances, which may pose serious safety concerns. Furthermore, the widespread adoption of self-driving vehicles could negatively impact the economy by displacing jobs in sectors such as taxi services, personal injury law, and driving instruction. Despite these drawbacks, the advantages of safety, efficiency, and accessibility associated with self-driving cars outweigh the potential risks, demonstrating that their integration into society could yield substantial benefits.

Safety is the most compelling advantage of self-driving cars. Research indicates that 81 percent of car crashes are attributable to human error. Factors contributing to unsafe driving by humans include distractions from mobile devices, driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, fatigue, and speeding. In contrast, self-driving cars rely on sophisticated algorithms and sensors that operate without the emotional and cognitive limitations of human drivers. This technological superiority significantly reduces the likelihood of accidents. However, it is crucial to acknowledge that self-driving cars are not without their own risks. They may struggle to navigate adverse weather conditions, such as rain or snow, and can sometimes misinterpret human traffic signals or gestures. Furthermore, a critical concern arises regarding the actions a driver must take if the technology encounters a malfunction. While such scenarios warrant consideration, they are relatively rare compared to the frequent incidents of human error that result in accidents. The data clearly illustrates that the potential for self-driving cars to save lives outweighs the infrequent risks associated with their operation.

In terms of efficiency, self-driving cars offer numerous advantages that can positively impact both cost and time management. With a decrease in the number of accidents on the road, significant savings can be realized in terms of insurance premiums and healthcare expenses related to traffic injuries. Beyond financial benefits, self-driving technology allows passengers to utilize travel time more productively, whether that involves working, relaxing, or engaging in leisure activities. Moreover, in scenarios involving multiple self-driving vehicles, a phenomenon known as platooning occurs. In this process, cars communicate with one another to optimize speed and spacing, which helps alleviate traffic congestion and reduces the likelihood of hazardous situations. This coordinated driving not only saves time and gasoline but also enables vehicles to travel at higher speeds safely, further enhancing overall efficiency on the roads. In essence, the integration of self-driving cars has the potential to streamline transportation and alleviate many of the frustrations associated with conventional driving.

Accessibility is another critical benefit of self-driving cars, as they can provide transportation options for individuals who are unable to drive, such as those with disabilities or elderly individuals. For many disabled persons, dependence on others for transportation or reliance on public transport can severely limit their mobility and independence. Self-driving cars could revolutionize their ability to travel freely and access essential services, ultimately enhancing their quality of life. Similarly, for elderly individuals, concerns about safety can often lead to difficult decisions regarding their driving privileges. Self-driving cars present a solution that allows these individuals to maintain their independence without compromising their safety, thereby alleviating the anxiety that families often experience regarding their loved ones' ability to drive safely.

While the technology behind self-driving cars is still under development, the potential benefits they bring to society are profound. If the advancement of this technology continues successfully, we can expect a positive transformation in the transportation industry, characterized by increased safety, enhanced efficiency, and greater accessibility. The implementation of self-driving cars could serve as a catalyst for a more inclusive, efficient, and safer transportation system, ultimately benefiting society as a whole.

draft1:
I marvel at the ingenuity of our scientists and engineers in bringing us so close to the possibility of self-driven vehicles. 

Furthermore, I am a free spirit who cherishes her independence, including the freedom to go where she pleases when she pleases, without having to rely on other people, their schedules, their preferences and especially their notions about what I, as a woman of a certain age, should or should not be doing. All this makes me deeply empathetic toward seniors and the disabled who may not be able to drive, and for their sakes, I can deeply appreciate the value of a self-driven vehicle.

Moreover, since self-driven cars are likely to be electric, they could mean the end of our reliance on fossil fuels,  something deeply to be desired by all of us who are worried about the future of our polluted planet.

And yet, I find that something deeper, perhaps even less rational, but nonetheless ineluctable, keeps me from endorsing self-driven, at least for the present.

We seem happy to cede increasing portions of our lives to computers. In cases where computers are absolutely better than us at certain things, e.g. the analysis of large quantities of crucial health-related data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But 
I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them, in areas where they are not particularly better than we are. Driving is a case in point. You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. And I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious. Even so, there is a whole parcel of skills that are ancillary to driving per se. I mean the ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, the ability to be with others and jointly negotiate traffic so that all who use the streets, including cyclists and pedestrians, are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 

And then, there is the questions of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation?  You may argue that we are everyday and everywhere rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, nor can the cars of anyone else, and you're all,  every single one of you, headed for disaster with no recourse, where at least if you were at the wheel you could have attempted some solution. It is this removal of human agency that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which 
can be the source of virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. I do not wish to be thus reduced.

	Moreover, since self-driven cars are likely to be electric, they could mean the end of our reliance on fossil fuels,  something doubtless to be desired by all of us who worry about the future of our polluted planet. 
	And yet, I find that something deeper, less rational, but nonetheless ineluctable, keeps me from endorsing self-driven cars, at least for the present. 
	We seem happy to cede increasing portions of our lives to computers. Where computers are absolutely better than us, e.g. in the analysis of large quantities of crucial data, say health-related  data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them. Driving is a case in point. 
	You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. And I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious. Even so, there is a whole parcel of skills that are ancillary to driving per se. I mean the ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, the ability to be with others and jointly negotiate traffic so that all who use the streets, including cyclists and pedestrians, are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 
          Some claim that self-driven cars would obviate the need for public transportation, seeing this as something to recommend them. I disagree. Apart from the jobs lost, which I think most would see as a negative, there is the potential loss of public space, which after all, is what a bus, a subway car, or a train is. Like cities, where they are most in demand, forms of public transportation make for chance encounters with others whom we may otherwise never encounter. Cities, especially the larger ones, are rich in human variety, and one way to ensure that we limit our contact with this enriching variety is to sequester us in our individual self-driven vehicles. Sequestration of a sort is already far advanced. Most of us walk around mesmerized by screens and tuned in to our own individual soundscapes delivered via headphone. At home, this pattern of behavior continues, sadly. We are losing the ability to be with each other, whether that means having rich and complex and spontaneous interactions or simply occupying the same space, something that is not as mindless and mundane and trivial as it might appear, as psychologists and neuroscientists are beginning to understand. Self-driven cars, by eliminating the need for public transportation will only deepen this social atomization.
          The advocates of autonomous vehicles claim that eliminating public transportation cuts infrastructure costs, and they might even say, it would make for greener environs. But it is not clear whether the rise in the number of autonomous vehicles will not require comparable infrastructure, as the majority must drive such vehicles if they are to be a feasible option in the first place.
         And speaking of majorities, we are brought face to face with another problem: cost. It will surely be a while before self-driven cars are within the means of the majority. And assuming that they could become a viable proposition before the majority of the population owns them, they would in the interim be status symbols, affordable only by the wealthy.  Our already deeply divided society can do without such obvious markers of status differentials.
	And then, there is the question of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation? 
	You may argue that we are daily rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. 
	I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, something as simple as a failure of traffic signals or a sudden thunderstorm, never mind a hacked program or a spectacular terrorist attack. Nor can the cars of anyone else. And you're all headed for disaster with no recourse. Rendered utterly passive where once you might have attempted some solution, no matter its efficacy. 
	It is this removal of human agency, individual and collective,  that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but rather thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which can breed virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. 
	I do not wish to be thus reduced.

output_text:
I marvel at the ingenuity of our scientists and engineers in bringing us so close to the possibility of self-driven vehicles. The advancements in technology are nothing short of astounding, showcasing human creativity and ambition. However, as we stand on the brink of this new frontier, I feel compelled to examine the implications of surrendering our autonomy to these machines.

Furthermore, I am a free spirit who cherishes her independence, including the freedom to go where she pleases when she pleases, without having to rely on other people, their schedules, their preferences, and especially their notions about what I, as a woman of a certain age, should or should not be doing. This desire for independence shapes my perspective on self-driving cars. While they promise convenience, I cannot overlook the loss of control that comes with them. All this makes me deeply empathetic toward seniors and the disabled who may not be able to drive, and for their sakes, I can deeply appreciate the value of a self-driven vehicle. Yet, I worry that while self-driving cars may enhance mobility for some, they could simultaneously diminish the freedom that comes from personal agency for many others.

Moreover, since self-driven cars are likely to be electric, they could herald the end of our reliance on fossil fuels, a shift that many of us who are concerned about the future of our polluted planet see as deeply desirable. The environmental benefits of reducing emissions and promoting sustainable energy sources cannot be overstated. However, the transition to self-driving vehicles must be handled with care, ensuring that environmental progress does not come at the cost of personal freedoms and societal connections.

And yet, I find that something deeper, perhaps even less rational, but nonetheless ineluctable, keeps me from endorsing self-driving cars, at least for the present. We seem happy to cede increasing portions of our lives to computers. In cases where computers are demonstrably superior, such as the analysis of large quantities of crucial health-related data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them, particularly in areas where they are not necessarily better than we are. Driving is a case in point. You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious; however, it encompasses a range of skills that extend beyond simply operating a vehicle.

The ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, to anticipate and respond to the actions of other drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians, is a complex interplay of judgment and instinct. These skills enable us to negotiate the shared space of our roads effectively, ensuring that all who use the streets are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, where human interactions are often reduced to screens and algorithms, these capacities are not merely useful; they are essential to fostering a sense of community and trust among us.

And then, there are the questions of safety that cannot be overlooked. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible; they can be susceptible to viruses, hardware malfunctions, and, most alarmingly, the vulnerabilities of hacking. A human driver possesses a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, or even a more sinister threat such as a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, algorithms capable of functioning effectively in highly dynamic environments without human intervention are still in their infancy. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech-savvy, in a complex and fluid situation? 

You may argue that we are already rendered helpless by the actions of drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree with the notion that human error is a significant factor in road safety. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. I implore you to imagine a scenario where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, nor can the cars of anyone else on the road, and you are all, every single one of you, headed for disaster with no recourse. In such a moment, where a human driver could have attempted a solution, the loss of human agency is palpable and troubling. 

This removal of human agency is what I worry about most. It represents a form of helplessness that is not merely a natural human vulnerability but an unsettling subservience to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which can engender virtues like compassion and empathy, this thralldom is not generative; it is lifeless, reminiscent of an inorganic thing. I do not wish to be thus reduced, stripped of my ability to make decisions in the face of uncertainty. I believe that our capacity to engage with the world around us—actively and thoughtfully—is a fundamental aspect of our humanity that we must strive to preserve, even as we embrace the marvels of modern technology.

In today's society, we are seeing the start of a new era in technology where artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. Examples of this can be found in our homes, social media, everyday retail, and other facets of our lives. 
	Many dystopian books and films would like you to believe that the steady integration of AI is the start of our societal downfall, but this is simply not the case--take self-driving cars for example. For those of you who are unfamiliar, self-driving cars are vehicles that rely on AI to give itself directions and to literally drive itself without any human interaction. 
	In general, this is a great improvement to driving safety as it can provide a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible--they get distracted, they get sleepy, and they can choose to drive while under the influence--which can drastically increases the chance of accidents. Computers, however, are literally unable to succumb to such mistakes and are therefore safer. 
	Additionally, computers are able to communicate with other computers easily, unlike humans who have no form of communication with any of the other drivers in close proximity. This advantage allows computers in self-driving cars to sense each other within seconds and negotiate the road together. 
	However, many people think that if we were to rely on these AI-driven cars, it would become a gradual demise for us. Self-driving cars are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on people en masse to "opt-in" and buy their own self-driving cars, otherwise a single self-driving car cannot communicate with a vehicle which cannot "talk back."
	But what these naysayers are forgetting is that all of these downfalls can be remedied with time--we are only seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation. It is a natural process to introduce new technology into society and wait for newer models to come out until it is perfected. By dismissing the new technology in its early stages without giving it the chance to improve with time would be like telling a child to stop practicing at something because they were awful in the beginning. 
	Here is a simple example of how we can slowly rely on technology to assist us: traffic lights. Way before we relied on traffic lights to help guide us through intersections, we had traffic guards to help us. But over time, we allowed ourselves to accept traffic lights, instead of a human, to help us through. And of course, the traffic light wasn't perfect or even similar to the version we have today! It also had a simpler code and fewer systems to help us, but we let it stay up and now we have near-perfect traffic light controls and coordination systems. 
	The point is, the more we work on this technology, the safer it will become, but only if we give it a chance to grow and perfect itself. This is not to say that the negative points aren't valid or accurate observations on the current self-driving cars we have today. But we tend to forget that this is not an indication of what it could be. Of what it could provide for us. And how helpful and much safer it could actually be for us in the long-term. 
        Manufacturers, scientists, and computer scientists alike will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. Will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily. And with further improvement, the more common the technology will become and cause its pricepoint to decrease, which in turn will make self-driving cars much more accessible and allow a wider population to purchase their own.
	There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology, and then it decides to just stop working or turn against us?" To that, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot feature in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we give cars the knowledge of the road does not mean that all of a sudden it is going to drive off without us.
	But I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever, and if you are one of the few who miss such a time, then maybe self-driving cars were never meant for you to begin with.

output_text:
Today's society is witnessing the dawn of a new technological era, marked by the increasing integration of artificial intelligence into our daily lives. This shift includes the gradual adoption of self-driving cars, vehicles that utilize sophisticated computer programming and AI algorithms to navigate our roads with minimal human intervention.

The potential benefits of self-driving cars are vast, particularly in terms of driving safety. Statistics reveal that human error is a leading cause of traffic accidents, as factors such as distraction, fatigue, and impairment significantly increase the likelihood of collisions. In contrast, autonomous vehicles are programmed to operate without these vulnerabilities. They are designed to maintain focus, respond to changing road conditions in real time, and make calculated decisions based on an array of sensory inputs. This capability positions self-driving cars as a potentially safer alternative to traditional vehicles, as they can analyze situations and react in fractions of a second—far quicker than a human driver could.

Moreover, self-driving cars possess the ability to communicate seamlessly with one another through vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) technology. This interconnectedness allows them to share critical information, such as speed, direction, and potential hazards, creating a network that enhances overall road safety. Imagine a scenario where several autonomous vehicles are traveling in close proximity; they can collectively optimize their routes, reduce traffic congestion, and minimize the risk of accidents by anticipating each other's movements. This level of coordination could revolutionize urban transportation, leading to smoother traffic flow and reduced travel times.

However, it is crucial to acknowledge that self-driving technology is not infallible. Concerns about security vulnerabilities persist, as these systems can be susceptible to hacking or manipulation. Instances of software bugs or unexpected system failures could lead to grave consequences. Additionally, the transition to a fully autonomous driving environment hinges on widespread adoption; if only a fraction of vehicles on the road are self-driving, the potential for accidents persists due to human-operated vehicles and their unpredictable behaviors.

That said, many of these challenges can be addressed through ongoing research and development. We are currently at the forefront of a transformative phase in automation, and history has shown that society has a long-standing tradition of embracing technological advancements. As we continue to refine self-driving technology, we can anticipate significant improvements in safety protocols, software updates to combat vulnerabilities, and enhanced algorithms for spatial awareness and adaptability. Over time, as the technology matures, we can expect the cost of self-driving cars to decrease, making them more accessible to a broader demographic.

Concerns about a dystopian future fueled by over-reliance on technology are common. Questions arise: "What if we become too dependent on our technology, and it fails us?" To those concerns, I would assert that science fiction remains just that—fiction. Our educational systems and training programs will not vanish simply because we adopt new technologies. For example, just because we have access to calculators does not negate the necessity of learning mathematical principles. Similarly, the existence of autopilot systems in aviation does not diminish the importance of training pilots to manually operate aircraft. The introduction of self-driving cars will not eliminate the need for individuals to learn how to drive; rather, it will complement our existing skill sets and provide us with safer transportation alternatives.

In conclusion, while the journey toward widespread adoption of self-driving cars presents challenges, the potential benefits far outweigh the drawbacks. As we navigate this new technological landscape, we must embrace innovation and adapt to the changes it brings. And let us be clear: while the evolution of driving technology may render the stick shift obsolete, it is certainly a small price to pay for the promise of a safer, more efficient future on the roads.

draft1:
Society remains divided on the issue of self-driving cars. Some people think they’re great: they should reduce accidents, free up drivers’ time, and make obsolete the need for detailed knowledge of driving, thus letting students learn other subjects in school and allowing people who could never use cars alone in the past (like the disabled and elderly) more freedom. Meanwhile, others focus on the problems. Computers would not make decisions and adapt in the same manner as attentive human drivers, provide more security risks, and present a large potential for vehicle failure. I side with this latter group, and my main reasoning is that there are more weaknesses and things that can go wrong with a computer than with a human.
I’ve got a little story on computers who’ve been trained to act like they can drive better than humans can. In April my parents and I were in Florida visiting my grandparents. We had a rental car and my father was driving on the highway. A car in front of us was going far below the speed limit, so my father punched the accelerator in order to pass it. The rental car’s computer determined that he must have meant to hit the brakes and hit the accelerator by mistake. So, the car suddenly locked up on the busy highway and stopped, causing all passengers to be thrown forward and the car behind us to nearly rear-end us. There was no human error involved here. No distracted driving. Just a system that has been trusted and deployed into millions of cars on the road, activating without the driver’s knowledge or consent. This “feature” is just a taste of what interacting with full-blown self-driving cars would be like.
Autonomous vehicles pose even more risks when we move beyond considering bad software that only influences how the car interacts with the driver and begin to think about security and how the computer handles driving in varying environmental conditions and around other drivers. According to the Guardian, the self-driving cars’ computers would be a target for hackers. There are so many situations where a self-driving car could be led to cause accidents, especially if the human behind the wheel has never learned to drive or is too distracted or too trusting in the vehicle’s technology to intervene. A simple idea is that a hacker can encourage the cars to engage in a platooning pattern (where the cars line up closely behind each other like train cars), at which point the cars would be like a line of little ducklings ready to do the bidding of Mother Duck, leading them off the road or suddenly slamming on the brakes when the cars are very close and travelling at high speeds. 
Many proponents of self-driving cars act on the presumption that vehicle computers have an understanding of the environment around them, and in fact would be even more aware than a distracted human would be. Sure, if they’re equipped with the right sensors they’ll know the speeds of nearby cars, they’ll acknowledge the possibility that a child or deer could run onto the road at any moment, and they’ll probably monitor weather conditions. But in terms of anticipating the behavior of other drivers, they fall short. When I drive, I can look at other vehicles and anticipate their behavior. This driver is turned around looking at their kid in the backseat. This person’s truck has tree branches in the back that aren’t tied down. This person’s car has been rear-ended several times and the bumper is being held on with duct tape. Each of these pieces of information influences what I expect the driver to act like. A computer isn’t going to look at its surroundings and judge everything like this. As such, a computer will not be as well-informed or adaptive as humans, because we will never be able to anticipate all the different types of drivers that the computer would need to be ready to judge. 
Overall, there is no debate that a self-driving car would behave differently from a human-driven one. Much of this discrepancy is due to the fact that computers are endowed with the ability to be truly conscious and can be easily mislead or misinformed.
